g.data-narration: Welcome to learning Regular Expressions, powerful pattern matching tools essential for text processing and validation.
A regular expression is a sequence of characters defining a search pattern for matching strings based on specific rules.
Originally developed by Stephen Kleene in the 1950s, regex is now fundamental in compilers, validation, and data processing.
Metacharacters are special symbols with reserved meanings in regex. The dot matches any character, caret anchors to start, dollar to end.
Quantifiers specify repetition: asterisk for zero or more, plus for one or more, question mark for optional, and braces for exact counts.
g.text: Example: "go*d" matches "gd", "god", "good", "goood" | "go+d" requires at least one "o"

g.data-narration: Character classes in square brackets match any single character from the set. Use ranges like a-z, and caret to negate.
Anchors match positions, not characters. Use caret for start, dollar for end, and backslash-b for word boundaries.
Parentheses create capturing groups that store matched text for later use. Use question mark colon for non-capturing groups.
Greedy quantifiers match as much as possible. Adding question mark makes them lazy, matching as few as possible.
Email validation pattern breaks into local part, @ symbol, domain, and TLD. The pattern ensures proper format with anchors.
Phone number pattern handles various formats including optional parentheses and separators. Backslash-d matches any digit.
Date validation uses alternation for month and day ranges. Note: This validates format, not calendar validity.
HTML tag extraction uses character class negation to capture content between tags. Parentheses create capturing groups.
Log analysis uses capturing groups to extract timestamp and log level. The pattern anchors to line start for each log entry.
g.text: Log: "2024-01-15 14:30:45 ERROR timeout" → Group1: timestamp, Group2: ERROR

g.data-narration: Here's a quick reference: digit, word, whitespace shortcuts, common patterns for email, phone, date, and URL validation.
Remember: escape special characters, use anchors for complete matches, understand greedy vs lazy, and practice common patterns.
g.text: Tip: Remember regex validates FORMAT not semantic correctness. Practice building patterns step-by-step!

g.data-narration: Regular expressions are essential for pattern matching, validation, and data extraction across all programming languages and tools.
g.text: 🎯 Master regex = Powerful tool for text processing, validation, and data extraction!
